plan.contextChunk:
Until now we have considered only the case where the two cameras are
parallel, but in general the two images will be produced by cameras that
can have arbitrary orientations. Note that disparity of the smokestacks
in @fig-titanic (a) increases with depth, rather than decreasing as
described by equation (@eq-depthFromDisparity). That is because the
cameras that took the stereo pair were not in the same orientation,
related only by a translation.

Let's assume we have two calibrated cameras: we know the intrinsic and
extrinsic parameters for both. They are related by a generic
translation, $\mathbf{T}$, and a rotation, $\mathbf{R}$, such that their
optical axis are not parallel. Let's now consider that we see one point
in the image produced by camera 1 and we want to identify where is the
corresponding point in the image from camera 2. The following sketch
(@fig-epipolar_1) shows the setting of the problem we are trying to
solve.

![How can we identify the specific point $\mathbf{p](figures/3d_scene_understanding/epipolar_1.png){width="70%" #fig-epipolar_1}

Are there any geometric constraints that can help us to identify
candidate matches? We know that the observed point in camera 1,
$\mathbf{p}_1$, corresponds to a 3D point that lies within the ray that
connects the camera 1 origin and $\mathbf{p}_1$ (shown in red in
@fig-epipolar_3):

![The point $\mathbf{p](figures/3d_scene_understanding/epipolar_3.png){width="70%" #fig-epipolar_3}

Therefore, $\mathbf{p}_2$ is constrained to be within the line that
results of projecting the ray into camera 2. Remember that a line in 3D
projects into a line in 2D. There are two points that are of particular
interest in the line projected into camera 2. The first point is the
projection of the camera center of camera 1 into the camera plane 2 (the
point could lay outside the camera view but in this example it happens

elements: left camera (Camera 1) represented as a black sphere
right camera (Camera 2) represented as a black sphere
left image plane (rectangle) with green axes and 'p1' label
right image plane (rectangle) with blue axes and 'p2?', 'p2'?, 'p2?' candidate labels
three candidate red dots on the right image plane (possible p2 locations)
one red dot on the left image plane at label p1
arrows from each camera center to its respective image plane
curved green 3D arrow from Camera 1 to Camera 2 labeled 'R,T'
coordinate axes (green for left image, blue for right image)

interactions.teaches: Orbiting helps the user understand the true 3D relationships between the two cameras and image planes, clarifying that the two are not parallel.
Clicking on p1 or Camera 1 highlights the 3D ray corresponding to the matched point and its projection into the right image, demonstrating the geometric constraint.

demo_steps.title: Setup: Two unaligned cameras
demo_steps.narration: Here are two cameras with different orientations, related by a rotation R and translation T. Each camera sees its own image plane, and the point p1 appears at a specific location in Camera 1’s image. Notice that the cameras are not parallel, which breaks the simple left-right disparity rule you may have seen before.

demo_steps.title: From p1 to 3D ray
demo_steps.narration: Suppose you observe a point p1 in the image from Camera 1. This 2D point actually corresponds to a 3D ray in space, starting at Camera 1’s center and passing through its image plane at p1. Every possible 3D location for the observed point must lie along this ray.

demo_steps.title: Projecting the ray into Camera 2
demo_steps.narration: Click p1 to highlight its corresponding 3D ray, and see its projection onto Camera 2’s image plane. The potential matching point p2 in Camera 2 is constrained to lie somewhere along the resulting projected line — not just anywhere in the image. This is the foundation of the epipolar constraint.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Initial view should match the slightly perspective skewed angle, showing both image planes at an angle and clearly displaying both camera centers, with R,T arrow concave upward and spanning comfortably in the lower margin.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, including the tilt and perspective of the two image planes, relative positioning, and text label scale
inline_constraints.visible_ui: no overlays beyond possible subtle edge controls; all direct interaction happens via clicking/hovering meaningful scene elements
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips for camera, labels, and points; click popups for in-depth geometric explanations
inline_constraints.framing: maintain center whitespace and figure margins from the source; do not force-fill the iframe
inline_constraints.camera: camera angle and zoom must closely replicate the original figure’s 3D apparent view

interactionPlan.notes: For the demo, add and animate a semi-transparent 3D ray from Camera 1 through p1, and render its intersection with Camera 2’s image plane as a highlighted line/curve. Use subtle highlight colors (yellow or magenta) to avoid overwhelming the main color code. Fade in candidate p2 points as distinct dots when the ray is projected. Scene labels (Camera 1, Camera 2, R,T) should remain fixed and legible irrespective of the interaction. Ensure the interaction can be performed easily by clicking p1 dot or Camera 1. Use Three.js Line and Sphere for geometric primitives; text should face the viewer (billboard style).

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The red feature points are black (rimmed) instead of solid red in the rendering
All feature dots are the same size, whereas the left p1 dot is larger in the original
The aspect ratio and spacing between the cameras and image planes are not quite as wide as in the original
The axes in the left image plane (green) are rendered as 3D arrows instead of flat as in the original
A checkbox UI control appears in the output but not in the original figure
evaluation.notes: Most primitives and labels closely match, but color/size, axes style, and excess UI elements slightly lower faithfulness.
action_items: Use solid red for all feature dots with correct scaling for the main p1 point on the left.
Remove the extraneous checkbox control or hide it by default to match the reference still.
Adjust axes arrows to appear flat (not 3D extruded) within the image planes.
Increase the horizontal spacing between the two camera/image plane setups to better match the layout in the source.

feedback.rationale: The issues are all concrete implementation flaws—color, scale, axis style, and excess UI—while the concept and structure are correct, so only the generator needs refinement.
action_items: Use solid red for all feature dots with correct scaling for the main p1 point on the left.

Increase the horizontal spacing between the two camera/image plane setups to better match the layout in the source.
